critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression comparison for 30-day readmission prediction has a reasonable experimental structure with temporal splitting, leakage prevention, and an appropriate primary metric (AUROC). However, the design contains a fundamental confound that undermines the comparison's interpretability.

The RNN and logistic regression receive substantially different input representations: the RNN processes full temporal visit sequences with rich per-visit encodings (timestamps, diagnosis codes, procedures, visit types), while logistic regression uses a fixed engineered feature vector summarizing visit history in aggregate (counts, binary flags). This means any observed performance difference cannot be attributed to the model architecture alone—it may reflect the richer temporal input that the RNN receives. The fairness of the comparison would be improved by also testing logistic regression on bag-of-events or temporally aggregated features derived from the same sequence representation used by the RNN.

The dataset size (10,000 admissions over 24 months) is modest, and with the training window covering only 12 months, the training set contains approximately 5,000-6,000 admissions. For an LSTM with 64 hidden units per layer trained with early stopping, this sample size may be insufficient to reliably train a sequential model while avoiding overfitting, particularly if the positive class rate is 15-20% (yielding ~750-1,000 positive training examples). The design does not address whether the training set is adequate for reliable LSTM training.

Readmission prevalence drift is identified as a potential confound and is checked, but if significant drift is detected the design provides no mitigation strategy—it only promises to 'report this as a confound.' A concrete approach (e.g., calibration recalibration on validation data, drift-robust evaluation) would strengthen the design.

The manual chart review audit of 200 records is a positive quality control step, but 200 records from 10,000 admissions is a 2% sample. Systematic labeling errors affecting rare but clinically important diagnoses may not be detected at this sampling rate, and the audit plan does not describe stratification by diagnosis type or readmission status.
all_issues_raised: RNN receives richer temporal sequence inputs while logistic regression uses aggregate summary features, confounding architecture with input representation
Training set of ~5,000-6,000 admissions with 12-month window may be insufficient for reliable LSTM training given sequence complexity and class imbalance
Detected readmission prevalence drift has no mitigation strategy; the design only promises to report it without remediation
Label quality audit covers only 2% of records without stratification by diagnosis type, potentially missing systematic errors in specific diagnostic groups
No statistical confidence intervals or significance tests are specified for the primary AUROC comparison on the held-out test set
RNN receives richer feature inputs than logistic regression baseline, conflating feature richness with architectural advantage even with the proposed MLP ablation
12-month training window on a single hospital with ~10,000 admissions provides limited training data for LSTM convergence — sequence length distribution unreported
Class weighting in binary cross-entropy produces poorly calibrated probability estimates; no calibration adjustment step is described before evaluation
Manual chart audit of 200/10,000 records is insufficient to detect systematic labeling errors — only random noise is addressed
No assessment of whether LSTM sequence truncation at the 95th percentile length loses clinically important early visit information for long-history patients
Twelve-month training window is vulnerable to temporal distribution shift; protocol for handling detected drift is not specified
Information asymmetry: RNN accesses granular visit-level sequences while logistic regression uses only aggregate features, confounding architecture with data richness
Manual audit of 2% of records is insufficient to detect systematic label errors from cross-hospital readmissions not captured in the EHR
Class weight balancing applied to RNN but not clearly stated for logistic regression baseline, potentially confounding the imbalance-handling comparison
Non-sequential MLP baseline is included only as a confound control rather than a primary comparison, limiting ability to isolate sequential modeling value from neural capacity
